title: Hormuz Transit Collapses to 7 Vessels, EU Sanctions Stall on Greek LNG and Austrian Banking, and SPR Hits 40-Year Low
subhead: Only seven vessels crossed the Strait of Hormuz on the first day of the renewed US naval blockade, with no VLCCs or LNG tankers transiting. EU member states failed to agree on the 21st Russia sanctions package, freezing the oil price cap at $44.10/bbl. The US Strategic Petroleum Reserve fell to its lowest level since 1983.

sections.heading: Hormuz Traffic Drops to Trickle as US Enforces Blockade
sections.body:
Only seven vessels crossed the Strait of Hormuz on Wednesday, the first day after the US reimposed its naval blockade on Iranian ports, down from 13 the previous day, according to Kpler data cited by Reuters. No Very Large Crude Carriers (VLCCs) or liquefied natural gas (LNG) tankers passed through the strait at all.

Four empty vessels entered the Gulf — three small oil tankers and a dry bulk carrier for grains. The three vessels exiting carried liquefied petroleum gas, coal, and fuel oil. On Tuesday, a Suezmax tanker carrying 1 million barrels of Saudi crude exited the strait with its transponder switched off, Kpler data showed.

The US military said its aircraft fired on and disabled an empty oil tanker attempting to break the naval blockade of Iranian ports. US Central Command said strikes beginning at 1900 GMT on Wednesday targeted Iranian coastal defence systems and cruise missile storage and launch sites, designed to degrade capabilities used to attack commercial shipping in Hormuz.

Iran's Islamic Revolutionary Guard Corps said it struck US military facilities at Jordan's Azraq Air Base, including communications systems, a radar site, and fuel depots, marking the ninth phase of its Saeqeh operation. Jordan's armed forces said they downed three Iranian missiles. Air raid sirens rang out in Bahrain, where the US Fifth Fleet is based, and Kuwait said it was intercepting Iranian drones. In Iraq, Kurdish forces reported eight explosive-laden drones shot down over Erbil.
sections.whyItMatters: The near-total halt of commercial tanker traffic through Hormuz — zero VLCCs, zero LNG carriers — confirms the strait is functionally closed regardless of US naval escort operations. The US disabling a tanker attempting to run its own blockade also signals a new enforcement dynamic where even non-Iranian shipping faces military interdiction.

sections.heading: EU 21st Sanctions Package Stalled as Greece and Austria Block Agreement
sections.body:
The European Union failed to adopt its 21st sanctions package against Russia after three days of negotiations, forcing a one-week extension of the existing $44.10/barrel oil price cap on Russian crude until July 23. The deadlock centres on two disputes.

Greece is blocking the package over a proposed ban on transporting Russian LNG to third countries, which Athens says would disproportionately harm Dynagas, a Greek shipping company operating specialized Arc7 ice-class LNG tankers serving Russia's Yamal project in the Arctic. Dynagas is owned by shipping magnate George Prokopiou. Greek officials argued these vessels cannot easily be repurposed for standard LNG routes.

Austria is blocking over Raiffeisen Bank International, the country's largest lender, which several member states want targeted for facilitating capital flows to Russia. Vienna has consistently sought to shield Raiffeisen from restrictive measures, Bloomberg reported.

Lithuanian Foreign Minister Kestutis Budrys warned that domestic commercial interests are overtaking strategic goals. EU foreign policy chief Kaja Kallas said a 'plan B' is being prepared if consensus on the price cap remains elusive. The rotating Irish EU presidency has scheduled an emergency meeting of ambassadors to try to break the deadlock before the July 23 deadline.

Separately, EU member states did approve 250 individual asset freezes and travel bans targeting Russian operators linked to recent strikes on civilian infrastructure, bypassing the broader sanctions gridlock.
sections.whyItMatters: A single member state's shipping interests are holding up the entire EU sanctions architecture. The freeze at $44.10/bbl means the cap will not adjust upward even though global prices have surged — keeping the cap well below market rates but also preventing the mechanism from functioning as intended. The Raiffeisen dispute reveals the depth of European financial entanglement with Russia that even 21 rounds of sanctions have not severed.

sections.heading: US Strategic Petroleum Reserve Falls to Lowest Since 1983
sections.body:
The US Strategic Petroleum Reserve fell to 316.5 million barrels as of the week ended July 10, the lowest level since 1983, according to EIA data reported by MarketWatch. Stockpiles were down 3 million barrels from the previous week, continuing a drawdown that began with the release of 172 million barrels authorized in March under the IEA's coordinated 400-million-barrel emergency release.

The SPR was authorized by Congress to hold up to 714 million barrels. The oil industry has long assumed the operational minimum — the point at which it becomes difficult to pump out remaining oil — is between 250 and 300 million barrels. But the Energy Department told MarketWatch that the actual operational minimum, determined by cavern mechanics, is only about 70 million barrels, leaving approximately 246 million barrels of usable inventory.

Energy Secretary Chris Wright said in March the US had arranged to replace the 172 million barrels with about 200 million barrels within the next year — 20% more than the drawdown — through exchange contracts where companies must return oil plus a premium. The Trump administration exchanged barrels released from the SPR for lower-cost barrels to be delivered back to the reserve starting early 2027.

TradeStation's David Russell warned that 'SPR draws can't continue forever,' while analyst Michael Lynch said that as the reserve gets lower, 'traders are going to worry that oil will not be available, even if the technical minimum hasn't been reached.'
sections.whyItMatters: The SPR at 316.5 million barrels — less than half its authorized capacity — means the US has far less emergency cushion than at any point in four decades. The DOE's claimed 70-million-barrel operational minimum is far below industry estimates and, if accurate, leaves more headroom than markets expect — but extraction difficulty increases as levels drop, and the exchange contracts for replenishment depend on prices stabilizing.

sections.heading: US Approves $1.96 Billion Weapons Sale to Saudi Arabia as Gulf Rearms
sections.body:
The US State Department approved an estimated $1.96 billion in weapons sales to Saudi Arabia, stating the sale would support 'the foreign policy and national security objectives of the United States by improving the security of a major non-NATO ally that is a force for political stability and economic progress in the Gulf Region.'

The approval came on the same day the US launched a new wave of strikes against Iran and reimposed its naval blockade. Saudi Arabia's Gulf oil loadings have slumped amid Iranian attacks on supertankers in Hormuz, though the exact figures are behind Bloomberg's paywall. Saudi Arabia's Yanbu terminal on the Red Sea has been running near maximum capacity as the kingdom reroutes exports away from the Gulf.

Iran's IRGC claimed it targeted the US Fifth Fleet headquarters in Bahrain as part of its ninth-phase Saeqeh operation, and Iran also claimed drone strikes on the Azraq Air Base in Jordan. Jordan intercepted three missiles. The spread of Iranian retaliatory strikes to Bahrain, Kuwait, and Jordan — all hosts to US military facilities — represents a widening of the conflict's geographic footprint beyond the immediate Gulf theatre.
sections.whyItMatters: The $1.96 billion arms package signals Washington's intent to deepen Gulf military dependency at a moment when Iran is directly striking US-allied infrastructure across the Arabian Peninsula and Levant. The widening strike geography — from the Gulf to Jordan and Iraq — means the conflict's energy risk premium is no longer confined to Hormuz shipping.

sections.heading: Senate Russia Sanctions Bill Targets India and China With 100% Tariffs
sections.body:
A bipartisan group of US senators introduced the Sanctioning Russia Act of 2026, replacing an earlier draft that proposed 500% tariffs on all Russian energy buyers with a revised mechanism targeting the top five purchasers — China, India, Slovakia, Hungary, and Azerbaijan — with tariffs of up to 100%, according to Firstpost.

The bill mandates sanctions within 30 days against Russian President Putin, senior officials, state-owned enterprises, financial institutions, energy projects including Yamal LNG and Arctic LNG, oligarchs, and Russia's shadow tanker fleet. It removes earlier language making sanctions contingent on peace negotiations, making many penalties mandatory.

The legislation exempts 15 European countries that import less than 15% of Russia's total gas exports and are taking steps to reduce dependence. India has accused the US of 'double standards,' with a senior Indian official telling The Economic Times: 'These are double standards vis-a-vis Europe and may not achieve the desired result. Making India a punching bag for US foreign policy failures will be counterproductive.' India imports over 88% of its crude oil from overseas and has significantly increased Russian crude purchases since 2022.

The bill carries added political weight following the death of Senator Lindsey Graham, one of its chief architects, with Democratic Leader Chuck Schumer calling for an immediate floor vote. Trump said the legislation has 'a good chance' of passing but suggested adding Iran and Hezbollah sanctions. It faces House opposition over the breadth of presidential waiver authority.
sections.whyItMatters: The bill represents the most consequential US sanctions legislation on Russia since 2022, but its targeting of India — a country buying discounted Russian crude partly because Hormuz disruptions have constrained Middle East supply — creates a policy contradiction: penalizing a nation for seeking supply alternatives to a chokepoint the US itself is blockading.

sections.heading: Iran Conflict Driving New US LNG Investment as Global Gas Supply Reshuffles
sections.body:
The closure of the Strait of Hormuz and the shutdown of Qatar's Ras Laffan LNG facility have prompted a significant increase in investments in US LNG export infrastructure, according to S&P Global reported by Bloomberg Markets. Asian and European buyers are increasingly turning to US LNG for long-term supply security as Gulf shipments become unreliable.

Goldman Sachs estimated that Gulf exports recovered to more than 80% of pre-war levels after the June US-Iran memorandum of understanding but slipped back below 50%, or about 11 million bpd, over the last week. The bank said Brent could exceed $110 in the fourth quarter if the Gulf export recovery continues to stall.

Meanwhile, Iran's IRGC warned it may use Houthi allies in Yemen to shut the Bab el-Mandeb strait, the gateway to the Red Sea and Suez Canal — which would put two of the world's most vital energy arteries under simultaneous threat. Analysts cited by Reuters said the threat is being taken seriously by shipping companies already avoiding Hormuz.
sections.whyItMatters: US LNG investment is the structural winner of the Hormuz crisis, but the Bab el-Mandeb threat means the redirection of LNG and oil traffic away from the Gulf could face a second chokepoint bottleneck. Goldman's $110/bbl Q4 scenario assumes only the current level of disruption — a Bab el-Mandeb closure would push prices far higher.

sections.heading: OPEC Cuts 2026 Demand Growth Again, Raises 2027 Outlook
sections.body:
OPEC cut its 2026 demand growth forecast by roughly 200,000 bpd from its June outlook to approximately 800,000 bpd, the cartel's monthly report showed, citing the impact of the Middle East conflict on global consumption. The reduction reflects lower demand in importing nations facing supply disruptions and price spikes.

In a notably divergent signal, OPEC simultaneously raised its 2027 demand growth forecast, projecting that demand will recover once the conflict ends. Exxon and Chevron have said current oil prices do not reflect on-the-ground fundamentals, with both majors expecting low inventories to keep prices elevated in the short term but anticipating a correction once Gulf flows normalize.

US PPI data released July 15 showed the biggest drop in 14 months in June, suggesting that energy cost spikes have not yet fully fed through to producer prices — a lag that typically takes 2-3 months to materialize.
sections.whyItMatters: OPEC's cut-and-raise signal — slashing 2026 demand but boosting 2027 — amounts to a bet that the current disruption is transitory. The risk is that the 2027 recovery depends on a conflict resolution that keeps receding: the June ceasefire lasted less than two weeks, and each escalation makes a return to pre-war flows harder.

bottomLine: The Strait of Hormuz is effectively closed to large commercial vessels, with zero VLCCs or LNG tankers transiting on July 16 and only seven vessels total making the crossing. The EU's sanctions machinery is gridlocked by Greek shipping interests and Austrian banking entanglement, freezing the Russia oil price cap at $44.10/bbl for another week. The US SPR has fallen to 316.5 million barrels, the lowest since 1983, with the DOE claiming a far lower operational minimum than industry assumes. Washington approved a $1.96 billion weapons sale to Saudi Arabia even as Iran widened its retaliatory strikes to Jordan, Kuwait, and Bahrain. The Senate's revised Russia sanctions bill targets India and China with 100% tariffs — a move Delhi calls a double standard when Hormuz disruptions are precisely what drove India toward Russian crude in the first place.
sources: Reuters (via USNews, AOL/MSN), Firstpost, MarketWatch/Morningstar, CryptoBriefing, Politico EU, United24Media, S&P Global/Bloomberg Markets, Goldman Sachs, Motley Fool, TNW, EIA, Kpler, US Central Command, US State Department, IEA
